ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indonesian bakso soup; 'meatball soup' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Meat soup) is the closest defensible match for bakso soup; no dedicated Indonesian meatball soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bakso soup or Indonesian meatball soup as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 bowl of soup with meatballs and noodles/rice
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of bakso soup is approximately 600 g, comprising a bowl of beef broth with several beef meatballs, a portion of yellow noodles or white rice, tofu puffs, bean sprouts, and garnishes of fried shallots and spring onions.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (Foodo); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for component-level estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB entry for bakso soup was identified. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against HPB Singapore data for similar mixed noodle/meatball soup dishes and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef meatballs, noodles, and broth components. Values are retained from the input as the best available composite estimate pending a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN match. Confidence is set to medium accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for noodle soup and rice-based mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bakso soup. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate components: yellow noodles (GI ~55–60, University of Sydney GI Database) and white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database). A weighted composite GI of approximately 65 is applied reflecting the mixed noodle/rice/broth composition at the 600 g serving size. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 45.0) / 100 = 29.25, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 7.5 × 6 = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from beef broth, soy sauce, and added salt; estimated 300 mg sodium per 100 g equates to approximately 1800 mg per 600 g serving, approaching or exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request broth served on the side, ask for reduced soy sauce, and limit the soup broth consumed to reduce sodium intake to a safer level per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~29 per serving) driven by refined yellow noodles and white rice; dietary fibre is low at 0.67 g per 100 g, insufficient to blunt postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced noodle or rice portion, substitute with lower-GI alternatives such as shirataki noodles or brown rice, and increase the proportion of bean sprouts and tofu to moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 1.67 g per 100 g (approximately 10 g per serving) from beef meatballs and fried shallots, combined with high sodium (~1800 mg per serving), presents a combined cardiovascular risk; trans fat at 0.08 g per 100 g is present but low.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request reduced broth to lower sodium, choose fish or chicken meatball variants to reduce saturated fat, and omit fried shallots to reduce overall fat and caloric load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef meatballs contain arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways; refined noodles and white rice contribute to high glycaemic load driving systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef meatballs with chicken or fish meatballs, retain ginger broth for its anti-inflammatory gingerols, increase cabbage proportion, and request plain tofu instead of fried tofu puffs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bakso Soup (Indonesian: Bakso Kuah; Chinese: 肉丸汤) is a popular Indonesian-origin meatball soup widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. The dish consists of seasoned beef meatballs — made from finely ground beef mixed with tapioca starch and spices — simmered in a clear, aromatic beef broth flavoured with garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and salt. It is typically served with a choice of yellow noodles, white rice, or vermicelli, and garnished with fried shallots, spring onions, bean sprouts, and tofu puffs. The broth is savoury and umami-rich; the meatballs are dense, springy, and mildly spiced. Per 100 g, bakso soup provides approximately 91.7 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat (1.7 g saturated), and 7.5 g carbohydrate, with 300 mg sodium. A standard 600 g bowl delivers approximately 550 kcal and 1800 mg sodium, making sodium management a key clinical concern. The glycemic load per serving is approximately 29 (medium-high), driven by refined noodle and rice components with low dietary fibre (0.67 g per 100 g). Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to high glycemic load and low fibre, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium. It is halal-certified in most Singaporean hawker and restaurant contexts. The dish is culturally associated with Indonesian and Malay food traditions and is a common hawker centre and food court staple in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of bakso?
Is bakso soup suitable for diabetics?
What are the ingredients in bakso soup?
Can I eat bakso soup if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from an ai_generated input record (confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central component-level data for similar mixed meatball soup dishes. No exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry for bakso soup was identified; values represent a defensible composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on dominant carbohydrate components from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is set to medium pending direct laboratory analysis or an exact approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for bakso soup is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this composite dish as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the input record and no exact approved-source entry was identified; estimated ~175 mg per 100 g was referenced in the input health suitability notes but is not confirmed by an approved source and is therefore excluded. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the input record and no exact approved-source composite entry for bakso soup was identified in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.08 g per 100 g from the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with beef-derived fat content. All core macros (protein_g 5.83, fat_g 4.17, carbohydrate_g 7.5) are non-zero and do not require zero-macro justification. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not resolve to the official HPB Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/); the nutrient source has been corrected to reference the official HPB Foodo portal. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for bakso soup is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024.
